_comment_for_translators: Romanian module strings. Keys with underscores are hierarchical UI strings. Keys that look like English sentences are inline phrase glosses (the English meaning of a Romanian phrase). Translate the values, keep the keys exactly as they are.
hero_subtitle: From zero to confident conversation. Five hundred words. Thirty-two core verbs. Sixteen real-life dialogues. A thirty-two-day pacing schedule.
hero_tooltip_help_prefix: Hover any Romanian text for its meaning and pronunciation (e.g.
hero_tooltip_help_suffix: ). Click to hear it spoken. Use the language selector at the bottom of the sidebar to translate the interface into your language.
footer_summary: Sixteen lessons · thirty-two verbs · five hundred-plus vocabulary · sixteen dialogues.
matrix_section_subtitle: Pick any verb. The grid populates with all nine cells — three tenses by three forms. Click any line to hear it. This is the engine the rest of the resource feeds into.
matrix_drill_body: Pick a verb. Read every cell aloud — twenty to thirty seconds total. Repeat with five more verbs. Do this five times a day at different times. Within a few days the structure fires automatically — that's the whole point of the matrix.
lesson_rules_subtitle: Read these before any grammar. They define how the rest of the book works.
lesson_0_subtitle: Before a single grammar rule, address the real reason languages don't stick — fear.
lesson_0_psych_q1: What specifically has stopped me from learning a language before?
lesson_0_psych_q2: Am I afraid of sounding foolish? Of being judged?
lesson_0_psych_q3: Do I believe I'm "bad at languages" or "too old"?
lesson_0_psych_q4: Have I been overwhelmed by grammar rules and textbooks?
lesson_0_psych_footer: If you said yes — you are normal. By Lesson 4 you will be speaking. By Lesson 16 you will be holding real conversations.
lesson_0_contract_body: "I will speak Romanian badly, bravely, and often — starting today." Write it down. Sign it.
lesson_0_why_body: The goal is a fireproof foundation — a structural skeleton so strong that even if you stop for months, it never fully disappears.
lesson_1_subtitle: Romanian is almost perfectly phonetic. Five special characters are all you need to learn.
lesson_1_avi_body: <0>â</0> appears <1>inside</1> a word (<2></2>). <0>î</0> appears at the <1>start or end</1> of a word (<3></3>). They sound <0>exactly the same</0>.
lesson_1_stress_body: Generally on the <0>second-to-last syllable</0> (about 80% of words). Unlike French, <0>every letter is pronounced</0>.
lesson_2_subtitle: Two short tables that sit underneath every sentence you'll ever say.
lesson_2_formal_body: Use <0></0> with strangers, older people, officials. It takes <1>voi</1> verb forms. Romanian formality is strict.
lesson_2_demo_body: Nobody says "această" in conversation. Use <0>ăsta / asta / ăla / aia</0>.
lesson_2_greetings_title: Salut! vs Bună! — who says what
lesson_2_greetings_intro: Among friends, family, colleagues, or people of similar age, Romanian uses two informal greetings — and which one you pick depends on the genders of the people involved:
lesson_2_greetings_formal: For anyone older, a stranger, or in formal settings, use <0></0> regardless of gender.
lesson_3_subtitle: Three tenses × three forms = nine sentence types. Master one verb across this grid and you can already say almost anything.
lesson_3_dont_panic_body: Start with just the <0>"eu" (I)</0> row. Once that feels natural, add <0>tu</0> and <0>el / ea</0>. Aim for <0>speed</0>, not perfection.
lesson_3_three_tenses_title: The three tenses you actually need
lesson_3_three_tenses_p1: <0>Future (colloquial)</0> = <1></1> + subjunctive verb. <0>Present</0> = the conjugated verb. <0>Past</0> = <2></2> + past participle. That's ninety percent of daily speech.
lesson_3_three_tenses_p2: Formal future uses <0><1></1> + infinitive</0> (e.g. <2></2>) — for news, books, and formal writing.
lesson_3_word_order_footer: Romanian often <0>drops the subject pronoun</0> — the verb ending already says who is speaking.
lesson_3_h_contracted: Contracted forms — how Romanians actually speak
lesson_3_contracted_h_negation: Negation + auxiliary — the most frequent contractions:
lesson_3_contracted_h_pronoun: Pronoun + auxiliary — almost always contracted in speech:
lesson_3_contracted_h_real: How they sound in real sentences:
lesson_3_drill_title: Drill — build sentences from the matrix
lesson_3_drill_body: Pick any verb. Run it through all nine cells aloud. Twenty to thirty seconds per verb. Repeat five times daily, ideally at different times. Within two or three days, the structure fires automatically — that's the whole point.
lesson_4_title: A fi — the most important verb
lesson_4_subtitle: "A fi" is irregular but indispensable: identity, descriptions, professions, locations, states.
lesson_4_shortcut_body: In speech, <0>este</0> shrinks to <0>e</0>. So <1></1> means the same as <2></2>
lesson_5_title: A avea — possession, age, and the past-tense helper
lesson_5_subtitle: "A avea" expresses possession AND is the auxiliary that builds every past tense.
lesson_5_imi_place_footer: Singular object → <0>place</0>. Plural object → <0>plac</0>. Past → <0>a plăcut</0>.
lesson_5_perfect_body: Conjugated form of <0>a avea</0> + past participle.
lesson_6_title: The verbs that cover daily life
lesson_6_subtitle: Infinitive, the "eu" form, the "el / ea" form, and the past participle. Drill each one through the matrix.
lesson_6_intro: Memorise these and you have access to almost any everyday situation. Hover any form to see its meaning, click to hear it.
lesson_7_subtitle: Three genders. The definite article attaches to the END of the word — unique among Romance languages.
lesson_7_h_def: Definite articles (the) — attached to the end
lesson_7_gender_body: Consonant ending → usually masculine. <0>-ă</0> or <0>-e</0> → usually feminine. Neuter behaves like masculine in the singular and feminine in the plural.
lesson_8_subtitle: Adjectives agree with the noun in gender and number. Possessives come AFTER the noun.
lesson_9_title: Counting, telling time, talking about dates
lesson_9_subtitle: The trickiest part is the teens — and the rule that puts 'de' before nouns when the number is twenty or higher.
lesson_9_built_body: 11–19 literally mean "X toward ten" → number + <0>spre</0> + <0>zece</0>. Some forms contract: <1>paisprezece</1> (not <1>patrusprezece</1>), <1>șaisprezece</1> (not <1>șasesprezece</1>). This is the trickiest part of Romanian numbers — memorise them.
lesson_9_twelve_body: <0><1></1></0> is masculine; <0><2></2></0> is feminine. Use the feminine when counting feminine nouns and when telling time — <3></3>. (12 is the only number 11–19 with both forms.)
lesson_9_de_intro: After numbers <0>20 and above</0>, insert <0>de</0> before the noun:
lesson_10_subtitle: A small set of prepositions does most of the work. Learn the "în vs la" distinction first.
lesson_11_title: Can, must, should — and how to compare
lesson_11_subtitle: Modals all use the same structure: modal + să + verb. Comparatives use 'mai' and 'decât'.
lesson_12_title: "I was doing / I used to do"
lesson_12_subtitle: Ongoing or habitual past actions. The imperfect is regular — even irregular verbs behave predictably here.
lesson_12_pattern_body: Take the verb stem and add the endings <0>-am, -ai, -a, -am, -ați, -au</0>. Works for almost every verb in the language.
lesson_12_drill_body: Complete these sentences about your own childhood. Read aloud, three times each.
lesson_13_subtitle: Two forms: "tu" (one person, informal) and "voi" (plural — also used with formal "dumneavoastră").
lesson_14_subtitle: Romanian uses the subjunctive where English uses the infinitive: "I want to go" → "Vreau să merg."
lesson_14_when_body: After: <0>vreau</0> (I want), <0>trebuie</0> (must), <0>pot</0> (I can), <0>o să</0> (will), <0>hai</0> (let's), <0>sper</0> (I hope), <0>cred</0> (I think). Only the <0>third person</0> (he / she / they) takes a special form — the rest mostly look like the present.
lesson_15_subtitle: Conditional uses 'aș / ai / ar / am / ați / ar' + verb. Three types of 'if' sentences cover almost everything.
lesson_15_if_title: Three types of 'if' sentences — and their English equivalents
lesson_15_if_past_pattern: If + past perfect, would have + verb
lesson_16_subtitle: The polish layer: how Romanians actually connect ideas, react, and reach across to English.
lesson_16_h_fillers: Filler words — how Romanians actually speak
lesson_16_h_cognates: The cognate trick — a thousand free words
lesson_16_mie_title: How to say I'm hungry / cold / afraid
lesson_16_cognates_body:
<0>-tion → -ție: information → informație, situation → situație
<0>-ty → -tate: university → universitate, quality → calitate
<0>-ture → -tură: nature → natură, culture → cultură
<0>-ment → -ment: moment → moment, apartment → apartament
lesson_17_subtitle: If you can render most of these into Romanian out loud, the foundation is solid.
lesson_17_test_title: Can you say all of these?
lesson_17_next_body: You now have the complete foundation. The most useful next step is exposure: Romanian YouTube, ProTV, Digi24, Romanian podcasts, native speakers. The skeleton you've drilled here doesn't fully disappear — even after months away, a few drills bring it back fast.
vocab_section_subtitle: The high-frequency words. Hover for translation, click to hear them. Read aloud, in chunks of twenty.
dialogues_section_subtitle: Read aloud — both roles. Cover the English column with your hand and try the Romanian first.
schedule_section_subtitle: A suggested rhythm. Adjust it to your life — but keep the daily micro-practice intact.
aboutme_section_subtitle: Fill in the blanks with your own life. This becomes your first real paragraph in Romanian — read it aloud three times.
aboutme_write_body: Replace the blanks with your own information. Read it aloud three times. This is your first real paragraph in Romanian.
lesson_15_if_real_intro: <0>Real / likely</0> — equivalent to the English <0>1st conditional</0> (<1>If + present, will + verb</1>):
lesson_15_if_unreal_intro: <0>Unreal / hypothetical</0> — equivalent to the English <0>2nd conditional</0> (<1>If + past, would + verb</1>):
lesson_15_if_past_unreal_intro: <0>Past unreal</0> — equivalent to the English <0>3rd conditional</0> (<1>If + past perfect, would have + verb</1>):
